Q1: My study routine was honestly a bit all over the place, but I tried to carve out time when I could. Being 27 and juggling other responsibilities, I found that listening to lectures and podcasts worked best for me—I could absorb information while commuting or doing chores. I'd aim for about two hours a day when possible, but some weeks were heavier than others. I kept a rough schedule in my head, but life often had other plans.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what was due and what else was happening in my life. Some weeks I'd be consistent, studying a bit each evening, but when deadlines loomed or work got busy, everything shifted. I'd cram more hours into weekends or late nights before assignments were due. It wasn't ideal, but I made it work with what I had.
Q3: Usually it was a combination of checking for new announcements and seeing if there were discussion posts I needed to respond to. I actually enjoyed the community aspect, so sometimes I'd log in just to see what others were saying. Deadlines were the biggest prompt though—I'd log in to download materials or check assignment requirements when something was coming up.
Q4: After a few weeks, checking the LMS became part of my morning routine, almost like checking email. I'd scroll through while having coffee to see if anything new was posted. The discussion forums actually pulled me in regularly because I liked hearing different perspectives. Once I got into that rhythm, it felt natural rather than forced.
Q5: The recorded lectures were absolutely essential for me since I'm an auditory learner—I could replay sections I didn't catch the first time. The discussion forums were surprisingly valuable too; hearing how other students interpreted concepts helped solidify my own understanding. I also appreciated when instructors posted audio summaries because I could listen to those multiple times while doing other things.
Q6: I tended to skip the lengthy reading PDFs, to be honest. My eyes would glaze over, and I'd retain so little compared to when I listened to content. Some of the supplementary materials felt redundant if the lecture already covered the topic well. I know I probably missed some depth by not engaging with everything, but I had to prioritize what actually worked for my learning style.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see how it connected to assignments or exams, or when it genuinely helped me understand something I was struggling with. The gamified tasks were actually engaging—they made learning feel less like a chore and gave me small wins when I was feeling low. If something felt like busywork with no clear purpose, I'd deprioritize it quickly.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't always afford time-wise, but when I did engage with them, it was more relaxed. Required activities came with this pressure that sometimes made them less enjoyable, even if the content was interesting. With optional stuff, I could explore without worrying about grades, which ironically made me learn more deeply sometimes.
Q9: Absolutely, I downloaded audio files and lectures to my phone constantly. I'd listen to them during my commute, while cooking, even while exercising. I also took handwritten notes from what I heard, which helped me process information better. None of that showed up in the LMS, but it was probably where most of my real learning happened.
Q10: There were entire weekends where I'd be reviewing my handwritten notes and re-listening to downloaded lectures without touching the LMS at all. I'd work through practice problems in a notebook, talk through concepts out loud to myself, or discuss ideas with a friend who was in a similar program. The LMS would show nothing, but I was deeply engaged with the material.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd download a lecture once, and then that file lived on my phone for weeks. I'd revisit it multiple times, especially before exams, but the LMS only registered that single download. The same with PDFs that I'd print out and annotate by hand—one access, many hours of actual study time.
Q12: There was a stretch mid-semester where I was just feeling really sad and overwhelmed. My attendance dropped because I couldn't muster the energy to show up, and assignments piled up. Work was demanding more hours, and I felt like I was failing at everything. It took real effort to climb back from that low point, but the gamified elements actually helped motivate me.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted or stressed, I'd log into the LMS but just stare at the screen without really absorbing anything. Competing responsibilities meant I sometimes had to choose between studying and sleeping or working. Confusion about assignment requirements also made me avoid starting things until the last minute, which wasn't great for my stress levels.
Q14: There were times I'd watch a video but my mind was completely elsewhere—thinking about bills, work drama, personal stuff. I'd get to the end and realize I hadn't absorbed a single thing. The LMS would show I watched it, but I'd have to go back and actually engage with it later when my head was clearer.
Q15: I definitely postponed the longer written assignments because they felt daunting. Even knowing they were important, starting felt like climbing a mountain when I was already tired. Sometimes I'd avoid discussion posts when I felt like I didn't have anything smart to contribute. The procrastination usually made things worse, but in the moment, avoidance felt like self-preservation.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my engine. I'd have bursts of intense LMS activity in the days before something was due—downloading materials, reviewing rubrics, posting in discussions. Without deadlines, I'd drift. They created urgency that I needed, even if it meant my engagement was uneven throughout the semester.
Q17: Feedback really mattered to me. When I got encouraging comments, I'd feel motivated to engage more and put in extra effort on the next assignment. Constructive criticism helped me focus my studying on areas where I was weak. But when feedback was delayed or minimal, I felt a bit lost about whether I was on the right track.
Q18: The course structure helped in some ways—having clear modules and deadlines gave me a roadmap. But the flexibility also meant I could fall behind if I wasn't careful. The discussion requirements kept me coming back regularly, which was good for maintaining momentum. I appreciated when the structure allowed for different ways of engaging with material.
Q19: The gamified tasks made engagement easier—they broke things into manageable chunks and gave me immediate feedback. What made it harder was when too many things were due at once, or when instructions were unclear. Also, having mostly text-based resources was tough for me as an auditory learner; more audio options would have helped.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is genuinely trying but struggling to complete work to their standards. Maybe they're researching, reading, preparing, but anxiety or perfectionism stops them from submitting. Or life circumstances might allow them to browse but not to sit down and complete substantial work. It doesn't mean they're not engaged—just that completion is blocked somehow.
Q21: That definitely resonates with me. I know my LMS activity didn't always reflect my actual study time because so much happened offline. Someone could download materials once and study from them for hours, or they might be a really efficient learner who doesn't need to revisit things repeatedly. Low activity doesn't equal low effort or low understanding.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you're active, what you click on, how often you return. But they miss the quality of that engagement entirely. They can't tell if you're genuinely learning or just going through motions. They miss offline study, emotional state, life context, discussions with peers outside the platform. It's a partial picture at best.
Q23: Being engaged means I'm mentally present and actively trying to understand and apply what I'm learning. It's not just about showing up or clicking through materials—it's about curiosity, asking questions, making connections. For me, engagement feels like investment, like I care about getting something meaningful out of the experience rather than just completing requirements.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how hard I fought to stay engaged when I was feeling sad and overwhelmed. It wouldn't capture the times I talked through concepts with friends or family, or how much I learned from just thinking about course material during quiet moments. It also wouldn't show how the gamified elements genuinely lifted my mood and helped me push through difficult weeks. My resilience doesn't show up in click data.
